figures.caption: Figure 2. Overview of the Facial Emoji Proxy Modeling framework. (a) Facial Emoji Learning Branch: 1) facial videos are abstracted into binary, identity-anonymized Emojis and 2) learn a VAE-based emoji manifold (Eϕ–Dϕ), 3) training coupled with emotion recognition task. (b) Cross-modal multi-task learning: the shared FMENet backbone (Eθ) encodes EEG into Z, driving two branches: ① Generative Branch maps to the emoji latent space and reconstructs dynamic emoji sequences via the frozen decoder Dϕ, and ② Discriminative Branch predicts emotion labels conditioned on the behavior-aligned code, grounding decisions in the learned behavioral manifold.

Figure 3. FMENet backbone, It encodes neural dynamics via two specialized modules: (1) Expression-Relevant Spatial Merger (ESM): discrete electrode coordinates are embedded by Fourier position embeddings to enable geometry-aware attention that aggregates channels into functionally coherent groups. (2) Multi-Scale Temporal Capturer (MTC): a stack of dilated 1D convolutions with exponentially expanding receptive fields captures spectro-temporal diversity, modeling both transient high-frequency responses and sustained low-frequency states.
Table 3. Ablation of key components in FMENet on the EAV and MMER datasets. We report accuracy and F1-score for the full model and its variants with individual components removed.

Figure 8. Data collection scenarios and research goal. (a) EAV dataset (Lee et al., 2024): active online communication with a cue-based conversational system, where 30-channel EEG and frontal facial videos are recorded while subjects engage in emotional dialogues. (b) MMER dataset (Yang et al., 2024): passive viewing of emotion-eliciting film clips, with synchronized EEG and facial videos and post-trial self-reports. We leverage these multimodal datasets to learn a dynamic mapping from incomprehensiblet EEG signals to facial expressions, enabling visualization of facial emoji proxies in EEG.
Figure 11. Video-level emotion annotation distribution provided by the original EAV and MMER dataset authors.
Table 6. Supplement to Tab.2 in the main paper. Performance (%) of different backbones on EAV dataset.
Table 7. Supplement to Tab.2 in the main paper. Performance (%) of different backbones on MMER dataset.

Table 8. Cross-subject EEG-based emotion recognition on the EAV and MMER datasets. We report accuracy and F1-score for EEG-only models and our proposed approach. All methods use EEG as the inference modality. ‡ denotes reproduced results using open-source implementations.

Table 9. Ablation on key architectural and sampling hyperparameters for EAV and MMER. We vary: (i) the output head dimension O (Eq. 2), (ii) the convolutional depth K of the MTC module (Eq. 3), (iii) the latent dimension L of the facial emoji decoder (Eq. 4), and (iv) the temporal interval ∆t represented by each emoji (§ 4.2).

Table 11. Computational efficiency comparison of different backbones on the EAV dataset. Darker orange or green indicates better performance for each metric (considering ↑/↓). Our method achieves the best recognition performance while maintaining competitive efficiency.
Figure 13. Extended visualization of representative examples from the EAV dataset.
Figure 14. Extended visualization of representative examples from the MMER dataset.
